title: Revisiting Transformer Basics and Rebuilding One from Scratch
content:
For the next few days, I want to revisit the Transformer from the ground up.
This post is the starting point for that review. The goal is simple: go back to the most basic concepts, make sure I really understand the moving parts again, and then build a minimal Transformer implementation from scratch instead of only reading diagrams or using a high-level library.
What I want to review
Token embeddings and positional information
Scaled dot-product attention
Multi-head attention
Feed-forward layers, residual connections, and layer normalization
Causal masks and padding masks
Training objective, optimization details, and basic evaluation

What I want to buildI plan to write a minimal implementation that is small enough to inspect line by line.

No unnecessary abstraction at the beginning
Clear tensor shapes at each step
Simple training loop
A few sanity checks on attention scores, masking, and loss curves

What I care about this timeWhen I review a model that I have seen before, the gap is usually not the high-level story. The gap is in the small implementation details that decide whether the model actually works.
This time I want to pay more attention to:

why attention is scaled by $\sqrt{d_k}$
how masks are broadcast
where residual connections are applied
how layer normalization changes training behavior
what can go wrong when reproducing the architecture from scratch

Expected outcomeBy the end of this small series, I want to have:

a clean set of learning notes on Transformer basics
a from-scratch implementation I can run and modify
a short record of mistakes, debugging steps, and experiment takeaways

I will start with the core attention mechanism and the minimum components needed for a working baseline.
